description: The neuron detects word beginnings or prefixes, particularly the initial few characters of proper nouns, technical terms, and common words at the start of sentences or phrases.

This neuron detects the end of comment or code block delimiters, specifically when transitioning from a delimiter marker (like "/*", "/**", or other formatting symbols) to the beginning of actual content.
This neuron detects the first letter or initial character of a word, name, or text token, particularly when it begins a significant proper noun, technical term, or content item.
This neuron detects formal or academic section headers and labels, particularly those that categorize or classify content in professional, scientific, or educational contexts.
This neuron detects uppercase keywords that are part of standard software license boilerplate text, particularly those appearing in Apache, MIT, and similar open-source licenses.

This neuron detects the suffix "-arean" in medical terminology, particularly in the word "cesarean" (also spelled "caesarean"), though it also responds to similar word fragments and some legal/technical language patterns.
The neuron detects the word "Invention" when it appears in the standardized header format "Field of the Invention" commonly found in patent documents and technical specifications.
This neuron detects specific legal/technical terms commonly found in open-source software license boilerplate text, particularly words that are part of standard disclaimer and warranty clauses.
The neuron detects YAML/LaTeX metadata fields in academic documents, specifically bibliography references and keyword sections that follow a specific formatting pattern.
This neuron detects the beginning of a text or code file, particularly activating on the initial byte-order mark (BOM) character or leading characters in file headers and formatted documents.
This neuron detects delimiter or separator tokens (like "~~~", "======", "<<", ">>") that are used to mark boundaries between different sections, comments, or text segments in a document.
This neuron detects the use of "\cdots" (a LaTeX command for centered ellipsis dots) in mathematical notation to indicate continuation or omission in sequences or lists.

This neuron detects the opening delimiter of XML/HTML declaration tags or PHP opening tags, specifically the "<?" or "<" character sequence that begins markup language declarations or processing instructions.
This neuron detects the keyword "pragma" in preprocessor directives, particularly when it appears at the start of compiler-specific pragma statements used for controlling warnings and diagnostics.
This neuron detects capitalized words that appear in legal or formal boilerplate text, particularly terms commonly found in software licenses and liability disclaimers.
This neuron detects the word "Appeals" when it appears in the formal name of an appellate court (e.g., "Court of Appeals").
This neuron detects the opening syntax of Apache mod_rewrite variable references, specifically the `%{` token used to access server variables in RewriteCond directives.

This neuron detects the plural noun "CONDITIONS" or "factors" in formal or technical contexts, particularly in legal boilerplate text (license conditions) and mathematical problems (prime factorization).
This neuron appears to detect page number markers or pagination indicators in legal documents and formal texts, typically denoted by asterisks followed by numbers.

This neuron detects the token fragment "prom" when it appears as part of words related to advancement, encouragement, or forward movement (like "promote," "promotion," "proliferation-promoting"), as well as in the element name "promethium."
This neuron detects the word "Unless" (or variations like "unless") when it appears at the beginning of conditional clauses that specify exceptions or requirements for preceding statements.
This neuron detects the word "title" when it appears as a metadata field label in academic papers or formal documents, typically in YAML front matter or bibliographic contexts.

This neuron appears to detect the "External links" section heading in Wikipedia-style articles and similar reference documents, specifically firing on the word "links" when it appears in this standard formatting context.
The token "schemas" appearing in XML namespace declarations or database schema references, typically in the context of Android XML layouts or SQL queries.
The neuron detects expressions of gratitude at the end of forum posts, questions, or technical discussions, typically appearing before or after a signature or closing statement.
This neuron detects the word "Supreme" when it appears as part of the formal title of a court, particularly in legal case citations and court documents.

This neuron detects words that are part of standard legal or formal language, particularly terms from software licenses and legal documents that describe conditions, requirements, or applicability of rules.

This neuron detects the prefix "hyper-" in medical and scientific terminology, particularly in compound words describing excessive or abnormal conditions (like hyperchloremic, hyperparathyroidism) or intensified states (hyperactivation, hyperchromatic).
